input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10 [th]-scale autonomous teleoperated vehicle equipped with advanced sensor and control technology. Its architecture is partitioned into a lower and upper level chassis, and a set of autonomous elements that provide perception, planning, and actuation functions. The vehicle operates in a controlled test environment where its sensors continuously stream data (e.g., vision, lidar, radar) to the Digital Twin, making it a suitable candidate for digital twin paradigms due to its high sensor richness, real‑time data streams, and the need to offload compute from the resource‑constrained ECU to external resources.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin System (DTS) offers three core services: 1) *Data Monitoring* – a UI that visualizes real‑time vehicle state, projected driving trajectory, and detected object tracks, and allows users to save data and generate detailed reports; 2) *Computation Offloading* – selective relocation of the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) algorithm from the vehicle ECU to the DT’s compute cloud, which processes lead‑vehicle detection, speed calculations, and returns desired speed commands; 3) *Remote Management* – low‑latency remote control capabilities (enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, issue emergency brakes) with a UI that simultaneously displays state data. Target users are operators (for monitoring and remote control) and developers (for debugging and data‑analysis). The services collectively deliver real‑time visualization, offline processing, and safety‑critical remote command execution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires the physical vehicles and the digital twin to share a consistent notion of time, achieving synchronization accuracy of 100 ms. Data and command exchanges between sensors, processors and actuators are conducted via ROS 2 messages timestamped with a unified clock, ensuring that any latency between sensor acquisition, data preprocessing, and actuation does not exceed the 100 ms bound mandated for safety‑critical vehicular applications.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 Reference Architecture, implementing each Functional Entity (FE) as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. The Physical Twin Entity hosts the hardware resources (compute, sensors, actuators) and implements lower‑level automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking. The Digital Twin Entity gathers data‑management and control‑mechanism services, while the User Entity contains the User Interface FE, subdivided into 3D Visualization and Control Panel sub‑components. Each FE is deployed on a distinct hardware node, yielding a decentralized deployment model. Coordination is achieved through ROS 2 topics and services, adhering to the ISO 23247 entity‑based structure and ensuring clear ownership and modular communication across the twin’s functional layers.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data such as LiDAR point cloud, vehicle state information, motor and steering sensor outputs flow from the Physical Twin Entity to the Digital Twin. The raw streams are serialized, aligned, and units are converted by the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. The data is then passed through the ROS 2 intra‑process network to the Data Pre‑Processing FE, where the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking SW component transforms the point cloud into object tracks and a projected driving path. The processed data is transmitted over a Wi‑Fi network using UDP to the user interface for real‑time 3D visualization.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued from the Digital Twin via the Actuation FE components (Motor Actuation, Steering Servo Actuation FE). These commands are serialized and unit‑converted by the Data Translation FE, then transmitted over UDP/Wi‑Fi to the corresponding physical actuators. Feedback signals (e.g., actuator state) are routed through the same Data Translation FE and the ROS 2 network back to the Digital Twin for real‑time status monitoring and confirmation of command execution.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between software components occurs within a ROS 2 network; inter‑entity communication (between physical and digital Twins) uses Wi‑Fi with UDP on the transport layer. The documents do not provide explicit bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security requirements.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: No information on hosting platforms (cloud/edge/on‑premise), computational/storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, deployment models, or availability/reliability assurance is provided in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system conforms to ISO 23247 (Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing), adopting the reference architecture described in Part 2 of the standard.  It implements an entity‑based architecture comprising: 1) the Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) representing physical assets such as equipment, processes, or products; 2) the Data Collection and Device Control entity that gathers sensor data from and sends control commands to the OME; 3) the Digital Twin entity that digitally models the OME; and 4) the User entity that provides monitoring and control interfaces.  Functional Entities (FEs) within each entity perform specialized tasks such as actuating the OME, transforming raw data into human‑interpretable formats, or interfacing with end‑users.  Although ISO 23247 does not prescribe any particular implementation technology, the system may employ OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) as the protocol for data exchange and device control, ensuring interoperability with industry automation equipment.  No further certification requirements or additional compliance frameworks are documented.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: Security and safety concerns for the digital‑twin solution are derived from ISO 26262‑1:2018, ISO 15622:2018 and the 3GPP TS 22.185 v15.0.0 Release 15. The system follows the ISO 26262 functional‑safety life‑cycle: hazard analysis and risk assessment (HARA) identify safety hazards associated with the ACC module; safety goals and functional safety concepts are documented, and safety integrity levels (SIL 1–SIL 4) are assigned. For example, the longitudinal control loop of the adaptive cruise control is specified at SIL 3, with a dedicated safety continuity function that forces the vehicle into a pre‑defined “safe‑brake” state when a fault or sensor failure is detected. Testing follows ISO 15622’s test procedures, including acceleration‑deceleration profiling, fault‑injection experiments and performance data logging at 10 Hz. Cybersecurity is addressed per the 3GPP V2X security architecture defined in TS 22.185 (with references to TS 33.160). The twin uses a public‑key infrastructure (V2X‑PKI) for mutual authentication of V2X messages; certificates are provisioned via the V2X‑PKI, message integrity is provided by HMAC‑SHA‑256 and confidentiality by AES‑128/256 in GCM mode. Access to the twin’s data is controlled via role‑based access control (RBAC) with OAuth 2.0 tokens, and all authentication events are logged. Threat‑mitigation strategies include continuous monitoring for anomalous V2X traffic, replay‑attack protection via sequence numbers and timestamps, and rate‑limiting to defend against denial‑of‑service attacks. Safety‑critical modules share sensor data over a secure CAN‑FD bus with message encryption and redundancy; diagnostic tests and watchdog timers ensure fault detection and recovery. The overall safety case, which satisfies ISO 26262 requirement A.15.5.5, is reviewed by an independent safety auditor and updates are tracked through the system’s configuration management process.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,
